coredata.dc:title: Facile synthesis and photoluminescence mechanism of green emitting xylose-derived carbon dots for anti-counterfeit printing
coredata.dc:creator: Yang, Pei
Zhu, Ziqi
Zhang, Tao
Chen, Minzhi
Cao, Yizhong
Zhang, Wei
Wang, Xin
Zhou, Xiaoyan
Chen, Weimin
coredata.dc:description: The realization of long-wavelength emissions and thoroughly understanding of photoluminescence (PL) mechanism are still huge challenges to be addressed for further development of carbohydrate-derived carbon dots (CDs). Herein, novel CDs with excellent green emission were synthesized from a precursor comprising of xylose and m-phenylenediamine in phosphoric acid solution via a simple microwave-assisted hydrothermal method. The PL efficiency was dependent on the combination of different acids and the precursor as well as the synthesis temperature, while the PL properties were mainly related to the acid base environment of precursor. Optical performance and structural characterization revealed that the oxygen-containing groups and protonated amino groups were mainly responsible for the blue and green emission properties, respectively. Moreover, a multi-center fluorescent mechanism on the different PL performances of xylose-derived CDs was proposed and verified by further investigating the pH-dependent PL behaviors. Based on the switching between green and blue emission in different pH regions, the xylose-derived CDs solution was demonstrated to be a promising fluorescent ink for anti-counterfeit printing. This research open a door for value-added utilization of xylose, as well as providing a facile and green strategy for large-scale preparation of CDs.

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body mmlmath acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid orcid primabst ref specialabst

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Xylose-derived CDs ink for anti-counterfeit printing

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the synthesis of xylose-derived CDs and the application of anti-counterfeit printing. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
(a–h) PL spectra of different CDs samples from 1-CDs to 8-CDs. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
(a–h) Absorption spectra of different CDs samples from 1-CDs to 8-CDs. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
(a–h) Represent TEM images of different CDs samples from 1-CDs to 8-CDs, the inserts are the corresponding SAED images. (i–l) HRTEM of 1-CDs, 2-CDs, 3-CDs and 4-CDs.
(a–h) Particle size distribution histogram of different CDs samples from 1-CDs to 8-CDs. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
XPS survey spectra, C 1s spectra, N 1s spectra and P 2p spectra of 1-CDs (a), 2-CDs (b) and 3-CDs (c). (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
XPS survey spectra, C 1s spectra and N 1s spectra of 4-CDs (a), 5-CDs (b) and 6-CDs (c). (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
XPS survey spectra, C 1s spectra and N 1s spectra of 7-CDs (a) and 8-CDs (b). (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
Proposed fluorescent mechanism of the xylose-derived CDs. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
(a–i) 2D fluorescent matrix scan of 1-CDs obtained at different pH from 3 to 11. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) Dependence of PL intensity on pH (change from 3 to 11) under excitation at 360 and 440 nm. Emission spectra of 1-CDs solution at different pH values ranging from 3 to 11 under 360 nm excitation (b) and 440 nm excitation (c). (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
UV–vis absorption spectra of 1-CDs obtained at different pH from 3 to 11. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
Photograph of 1-CDs in different buffers of pH 3 to 11 under 365 nm UV light. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

QR code printed on paper with the 1-CDs ink under natural irradiation (a) and under a 365 nm UV lamp (b). QR code pattern coated with diluted sodium hydroxide solution (c) and dilute acetic acid (d) under excitation with a 365 nm UV lamp. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

head.ce:title: Facile synthesis and photoluminescence mechanism of green emitting xylose-derived carbon dots for anti-counterfeit printing

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Materials Science and Engineering, Nanjing Forestry University, Nanjing, 210037, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Fast-growing Tree & Agro-fibre Materials Engineering Center, Nanjing, 210037, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Fast-growing Tree & Agro-fibre Materials Engineering Center

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author.College of Materials Science and Engineering, Nanjing Forestry University, Nanjing, 210037, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author.College of Materials Science and Engineering, Nanjing Forestry University, Nanjing, 210037, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The realization of long-wavelength emissions and thoroughly understanding of photoluminescence (PL) mechanism are still huge challenges to be addressed for further development of carbohydrate-derived carbon dots (CDs). Herein, novel CDs with excellent green emission were synthesized from a precursor comprising of xylose and m-phenylenediamine in phosphoric acid solution via a simple microwave-assisted hydrothermal method. The PL efficiency was dependent on the combination of different acids and the precursor as well as the synthesis temperature, while the PL properties were mainly related to the acid base environment of precursor. Optical performance and structural characterization revealed that the oxygen-containing groups and protonated amino groups were mainly responsible for the blue and green emission properties, respectively. Moreover, a multi-center fluorescent mechanism on the different PL performances of xylose-derived CDs was proposed and verified by further investigating the pH-dependent PL behaviors. Based on the switching between green and blue emission in different pH regions, the xylose-derived CDs solution was demonstrated to be a promising fluorescent ink for anti-counterfeit printing. This research open a door for value-added utilization of xylose, as well as providing a facile and green strategy for large-scale preparation of CDs.

ce:section.ce:para: Since their first serendipitous discovery in 2004 [1], fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) have attracted tremendous attention owing to their multiple merits, such as superior photoluminescence (PL), high chemical stability, excellent biocompatibility, cost-effective preparation, abundant starting materials and low toxicity [2–5], Benefiting from their multiple advantages, these nanostructures have stimulated considerable interest in many fields, including bioimaging [6–8], fluorescent nanosensors [9–13], photocatalysts [14,15], information encryption [16,17] and optoelectronic devices [2,18,19]. Recent advances in the fabrication of fluorescent CDs allow them to be prepared by peeling nanostructures from bulk carbon sources, such as graphite [20], carbon fibers [21] or carbon nanotubes [22], through top-down method; or, alternatively, synthesized from the carbonization of molecular precursors, such as carbohydrate [23], organic acids [7], or amino acids [8] via bottom-up route. Currently, enormous efforts have been made aiming to synthesize CDs by green chemistry methods for large-scale and sustainable applications. Motivated by this issue, carbohydrates are considered as one of the most promising carbon sources by their numerous virtues of abundance, renewable, high water solubility, low-carbonization temperature, economic and nontoxicity. Therefore, researchers increasingly focus on the advantages of carbohydrates when considering the synthesis of CDs with excellent properties. In the development of synthetic CDs, a variety of monosaccharides and their derivatives such as glucose [23–27], glucosamine [28–30], fructose [31,32], and common disaccharides including sucrose [33,34], maltose [35–37] and lactose [38–40] have been used for the preparation of CDs, Moreover, several research groups have also successfully synthesized CDs using carbohydrate-based biopolymers, such as cellulose [41], chitin [42], chitosan [9] and hyaluronic acid [43]. However, despite many efforts are underway to develop carbohydrate-based CDs, the optimal emission of most carbohydrate-derived CDs is in the blue-light region as compared with that of the CDs synthesized using other carbon sources, such as citrate acid [7,16,44,45], ammonium citrate [46,47] and amino acid [8]. In the view of tunable emission, the doping process has been confirmed to be an effective way to tune the chemical and electronic characteristics of the CDs, which is likely to narrow the band gap and thus result in long-wavelength emissions. Nonetheless, for the most of heteroatom-doped carbohydrate-derived CDs, their fluorescence efficiency is improved significantly, while the maximum emission peak suffer from the difficulty in red shift. As a matter of fact, the single blue emission of carbohydrate-derived CDs, which together with ambiguous luminescent mechanism have severely restricted their development and wider applications. Hence, the realization of long-wavelength emissions with high PL efficiency is highly desirable for the carbohydrate-derived CDs. To date, there have been only a few reports concerning tunable emission of carbohydrate-derived CDs from blue to larger emission wavelengths. For instance, Liu et al. prepared CDs with green emission through hydrothermal treatment of glucosamine with excess pyrophosphate [3]. Zhang et al. obtained yellow luminescent CDs by microwave pyrolysis of the mixture of glucose and concentrated phosphoric acid (H3PO4) [48]. Liang et al. adopted a microwave-assisted route to prepare green-emissive CDs with the raw materials of m-phenylenediamine (mPD) and glucose in the ethylene glycol solution [49]. Feng et al. fabricated fluorine-modified CDs from an ethanol solution of glucose by a two-step solvothermal approach [17]. Although the carbohydrate-derived CDs with long-wavelength emissions have been obtained in these pioneering works, there still exist certain problems, including complex processing, toxic reagent, long treatment time and the prepared CDs suffer from low PL efficiency. Importantly, the lack of thorough understanding of the PL mechanism is still a subject of scientific debates. Thus, it is still important and urgent to develop facile method for preparing CDs with long wavelength or tunable emissions, and gain insight into the intrinsic mechanism on the PL emission.

In this work, we report a novel one-step approach to the preparation of strong fluorescent CDs based on the pyrolysis of xylose and mPD. The low-cost and renewable xylose is used as carbon source for the first time, and the mPD containing nitrogen atoms can function as N-dopants for CDs. The xylose-derived CDs with excellent green and blue emission behaviors can be obtained by changing the reaction environment of the precursor comprising of xylose and mPD. Considering microwave heating-based synthesis is more effective than the traditional hydrothermal treatment in accelerating the reaction rate in an aqueous phase, a microwave synthesis system which can be controlled over temperature accurately is employed to synthesize xylose-derived CDs in one-step within just a few minutes. Furthermore, based on the experimental results, a mechanism model is proposed for the PL behaviors of the synthesized CDs, illustrating that the different emissions of CDs arise from multiple luminescent centers (LCs) controlled by surface defect states. The evolution of green emission to blue emission in different pH regions is also investigated carefully to validate the altering of surface defect states within the synthesized green-emissive CDs, the switching between green- and blue-fluorescence facilitates the synthesized green-emissive CDs as valuable fluorescent ink for optical anti-counterfeit printing and encryption of written information (Fig. 1 ). This research provide a novel pathway for the development of xylose-derived CDs with versatile functions in pursuit of promising application.

D-(+)-Xylose, mPD, quinine sulfate and Rhodamine 6G were all purchased from Aladdin Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). H3PO4, nitric acid (HNO3), acetic acid (CH3COOH), trisodium phosphate (Na3PO4) and sodium hydrate (NaOH) were procured from Nanjing Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd (Nanjing, China). Phosphate buffer solutions (PBS) of different pH values were obtained from Haibiao Technologies Co., Ltd (Fuzhou, China). All the reagents were used as received without further purification. Ultrapure water was used throughout the experiments.

The CDs were synthesized by a simple green route of microwave assisted pyrolysis method. Firstly, the precursor solutions were prepared by dissolving 0.1 g of xylose and 0.2 g of mPD in to 10 mL of ultrapure water, and then mixed with 200 μL of H3PO4 with vigorous stirring to form starting solution. Subsequently, the clear transparent starting solution was transferred into reaction tube and heated in the microwave reactor at 220 °C for 10 min. After the reaction completed and naturally cooled down to room temperature, a resulting dark brown solution was obtained, indicating the carbonization of reactant. The resultant product without a further adjustment of pH value was filtered by a PTFE syringe filter with pore size of 0.22 μm to remove large particles and dialyzed with a dialysis membrane (500 Da of cut off molecular weight) for 48 h to remove the unreacted small molecules. Similarly, another two types of CDs, which were labelled as 2-CDs and 3-CDs, were also synthesized at 200 and 180 °C using the same starting solution containing xylose, mPD and H3PO4, and the synthetic procedure was the same as above, except the microwave treatment temperature was set at 200 and 180 °C, respectively. To investigate the effect of acid on the PL performance of CDs, the H3PO4 was replaced by HNO3 and CH3COOH with the same volume amount to participate in the synthesis of CDs, respectively, and the other synthetic procedure was the same as that for the preparation of 1-CDs, the obtained CDs were denoted as 4-CDs and 5-CDs. The precursor in the absence of acid was also used to synthesize CDs under the same conditions as the sample 1-CDs, the resultant product was denoted as 6-CDs. Moreover, 0.50 g of Na3PO4 and 0.37 g of NaOH were added to the precursor comprising of xylose and mPD, respectively, the resulting mixtures were served as starting solutions, and the synthesis procedure was otherwise the same as that for the fabrication of 1-CDs, the CDs prepared from the starting solutions containing Na3PO4 and NaOH were denoted as 7-CDs and 8-CDs, respectively. For convenience, Table S1 of the supporting information shows the different conditions for the synthesis of CDs. Prior to further characterization, all solid CDs samples were obtained under vacuum freeze-drying condition.

The microwave synthesis system was commercially available and produced by Chemistry Electronic Microwave Company, (DISCOVER SP, USA). PL characterization were performed on an F-7000 spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan), the band pass for excitation and emission was set as 10 nm. Ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) absorption was characterized by a Lambda 950 UV–Visible spectrophotometer (PerkinElmer, USA). Time-resolved fluorescence decay curves were measured under excitation at 410 nm using a FLS 980 time-correlated single-photon counting system (EI, UK). High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) micrographs were obtained using a JEM-2100 UHR transmission electron microscope (JEOL, Japan) operating at 200 kV. Fourier transformed infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) was measured at wavenumbers ranging from 500 to 4000 cm−1 using a Vertex 80V FTIR spectrometer (Bruker, Germany). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was collected in an AXIS UltraDLD electron spectrometer (Shimadzu, UK) with a monochromatic X-ray source Al Kα excitation (1486.6 eV), and the binding energy was calibrated based on the C 1s peak at 284.6 eV. The surface charge of the synthesized CDs was measured using a ZEN3600 Zeta Plus (Malvern, UK) instrument with a He-Ne laser source. All measurements were carried out at room temperature under ambient conditions.

The PL quantum yield (PL QY) of xylose-derived CDs was determined by a well-established comparative method [50]. Quinine sulfate in 0.1 M H2SO4, rhodamine 6G in EtOH were chosen as the standards that have the fixed and known QY values. In order to minimized re-absorption, absorbencies in 10−mm quartz cell were adjusted to less than 0.05. The absorption values and fluorescent spectra of CDs solutions and reference solutions were measured and the PL QY of all CDs samples were calculated by the following equation:
(1)
Q
x
=
Q
s t
(
I
x
/
I
s t
)
(
A
s t
/
A
x
)
(
η
x
2
/
η
s t
2
)

Here, Q stands for PL QY. I refers to the integrated fluorescence peak area. A is the measured absorbance. η represents the refractive index of the solvent. The subscripts x and st correspond to the CDs samples and standards, respectively.

The resulting CDs exhibited interesting PL performance and were thoroughly investigated. As shown in Fig. 2 a–h, it can be seen that the maximum emission peak of CDs samples from 1-CDs to 6-CDs are centered at about 518 nm under 440 nm excitation, indicating their green emission properties. In contrast, the strongest emissions of 7-CDs and 8-CDs can be observed at wavelength of 455 nm and 435 nm, respectively, which suggests that these two CDs samples are mainly represented by blue emission behavior. Therefore, the CDs sample from 1-CDs to 6-CDs with green emission (denoted as green-CDs) and the CDs, including 7-CDs and 8-CDs, with blue emission (denoted as blue-CDs) were successfully synthesized using the same precursor comprising xylose and mPD in one step by a simple microwave-assisted hydrothermal method. Additionally, it is worth mentioning that the product synthesized only from xylose or mPD in H3PO4 solution had almost no fluorescent emission. Thus, the formation of fluorescent CDs should be contributed from the synergistic role of xylose and mPD, while the addition of H3PO4, HNO3, CH3COOH, Na3PO4 or NaOH to the precursor comprising xylose and mPD significantly alter the nucleation and growth process, resulting in the synthesized CDs with multiple functional groups that ultimately determine their PL properties.

For green-CDs samples, their emission spectra are similar in spite of different reaction system. In brief, the PL intensity of each green-CDs sample increased until the excitation reached 440 nm and then dramatically decreased with the excitation shift to longer wavelength. Thus, adding H3PO4, HNO3 or CH3COOH to the precursor comprising xylose and mPD did not play a vital role in the green emitting CDs. Furthermore, it should be mentioned that the emission spectra of each green-CDs sample was composited with two different fractions, as the excitation wavelength increased from 360 to 400 nm, the emission peaks gradually shifted from 513 to 518 nm, when the excitation wavelength was over 400 nm, the obtained emission spectra exhibited a typical excitation-independent PL behavior. In particular, the emission spectra determined at excitation wavelength of 360 nm and 380 nm displayed prominent broadening in the blue emission region, such emission characteristic was more distinct for 5-CDs and 6-CDs. These phenomena suggest that the PL emissions of green-CDs samples with excitation-dependent and excitation-independent characteristics could be related to the different LCs controlled by defect states.

Unlike green-CDs, the emission position and PL intensity of 7-CDs had almost no change when the excitation wavelength was adjusted from 320 to 340 nm. Accompanying the increase of excitation wavelength from 340 to 440 nm, the emission peak gradually shifted to longer wavelengths, while the PL intensity increased slightly at the excitation wavelength from 360 to 380 nm, and then decreased under excitation in the region at 380–440 nm. The 8-CDs exhibited a typical excitation-dependent PL emission performance when the excitation wavelength was increased from 320 to 440 nm, the PL intensity decreased gradually, accompanying with significant red-shift of emission spectrum. In most cases, the CDs with excitation-dependent PL characteristic is likely attributable to numerous energy levels within their structures [51]. On comparing the emission performance of 7-CDs and 8-CDs, it can be found that the 7-CDs and 8-CDs with emission maxima at 455 nm and 435 nm, respectively, at the same excitation wavelength of 320 nm. The possible reason for this difference is that Na3PO4 and NaOH in the reaction systems generated different defect states via the change of surface functional groups in blue-CDs.

Based on the abovementioned analyses, the precursor comprising xylose and mPD with and without adding acid is beneficial to fabricating green emitting CDs with two characteristic emission behaviors, while the alkaline environment of the starting solution played a vital role in the formation of blue emitting CDs with excitation-dependent performance. The relative PL QY of the green-CDs were determined to be 73.6%, 56.1%, 40.9%, 65.3%, 49.5% and 42.8% for 1-CDs to 6-CDs, respectively, as measured using the rhodamine 6G in EtOH as standard reference and with excitation at 440 nm. These results illustrate that the as-prepared green-CDs were possessed of excellent PL performance in aqueous solution. Meanwhile, the PL QYs for 7-CDs and 8-CDs were 8.0% and 6.8% under their optimal excitation wavelengths using quinine sulfate as standard. As revealed by above results, in spite of the same reaction system, the increasing PL QY from 3-CDs, 2-CDs to 1-CDs implies that higher reaction temperature was beneficial to enhancing the PL efficiency of CDs. Notably, although preparing at the same synthesis temperature, the relative PL QY of 1-CDs was higher than that of 4-CDs, 5-CDs and 6-CDs, indicating that H3PO4 as dopant in the precursor can significantly improve the PL performance of resultant green-CDs.

To further elucidate the optical properties of the CDs, the UV–vis absorption spectra of all CDs samples were carried out (Fig. 3 a–h). The absorption spectrum of each green-CDs sample exhibited a distinct absorption band centered at 280 nm, which can be assigned to the intrinsic state (π–π*) transition from the aromatic sp2 domains (C=C and C=N) [8]. Although at lower intensities, another absorption band appeared in the region at 385–440 nm is related to the localized conjugating structural unites and defect states in green-CDs [8,52,53]. However, the UV–vis absorption spectra of blue-CDs extended into the visible region with a single peak at 280 nm, which is commonly originated from the intrinsic state (π–π*) transition and multiple O-containing groups within the structures of blue-CDs [46]. Typically, the energy gaps in CDs are responsible for their different absorption band. Thus, the absorption band (385–440 nm) of green-CDs in lower energy region indicates that the heteroatom doping induced new defect states located in the π–π∗ gap during the formation of nanostructures. As a result, it can be deduced that the lower band gap induced by a uniform defect state is closely related to green emission and excitation-independent PL behavior, while the several larger band gaps derived from the multiple defect states should be primarily responsible for blue emission and excitation-dependent performance.

To further evidence the green emission and blue emission originate from different LCs, the fluorescence lifetime of each CDs sample was measured, then the obtained lifetime decay curves of all CDs samples were fitted well using a double-exponential function (Eq. (2)), and the average lifetime was calculated according to Eq. (3).
(2)
y
(
t
)
=
A
1
e x p
(
− t /
τ
1
)
+
A
2
e x p
(
− t /
τ
2
)
(3)
τ
∗
=
(
A
1
τ
1
2
+
A
2
τ
2
2
)
/
A
1
τ
1
+
A
2
τ
2
Here, A 1 and A 2 are the fractional contributions of time-resolved fluorescence decay lifetimes τ 1 and τ 2, respectively, and τ ∗ refers to the average lifetime.

As shown in Fig. S1 and Table 1 , the obtained average lifetime reveals that the radiative recombination of the excitons gave rise to the fluorescence. Furthermore, the established dual-exponential model reveals that the lifetime decay involves a short-lived component τ1 and long-lived component τ2 which can be assigned to the recombination process in the core states and the surface states, respectively [8,46]. Indeed, the short-lived component τ1 of each green-CDs sample was less than that of each blue-CDs, while the comparison of long-lived component τ2 of green-CDs and blue-CDs revealed the opposite results. Thus, the green-CDs and blue-CDs have not only different emission trap sites in the carbon core, but also different electronic transition process on the surface. Moreover, the comparison of τ1 proportion and τ2 proportion illustrates that the core states and surface states dominate the fluorescence decay of green-CDs and blue-CDs, respectively. The possible reason underlying the results is that the different PL emissions of CDs associate with the difference in the distribution of excitons between the interior and the surface.

The representative TEM images of as-prepared CDs samples from 1-CDs to 8-CDs are shown in Fig. 4 a–h, respectively. It is clear that all the resultant nanostructures exhibited different sizes and morphologies. Specifically, the quasi-spherical shape and excellent dispersibility can be observed for the 1-CDs to 4-CDs. In contrast, the as-prepared CDs samples from 5-CDs to 8-CDs demonstrated an irregular morphology, and the different degrees of aggregation can be observed in 7-CDs and 8-CDs. Furthermore, the selected area electron diffraction (SAED) was also used to characterize all CDs samples (upper inset in Fig. 4a–h), the well-defined diffraction spots and rings of 1-CDs, 2-CDs, 3-CDs and 4-CDs confirmed their crystalline nature, and the individual CDs in the high resolution TEM (HRTEM) images of 1-CDs to 4-CDs (Fig. 4i–l) displayed a clear lattice spacing of 0.34 nm, which is consistent with the (002) lattice plane of graphite. However, the SAED of 5-CDs to 8-CDs demonstrated their amorphous carbon structure. Thus, the participation of H3PO4 and HNO3 in the synthesis of CDs impelled dehydration reaction and carbonization process, resulting in the uniform morphology and crystalline structures of carbon spheres, while the addition of CH3COOH, Na3PO4 or NaOH in starting solutions had almost no effect on the dehydration rate of precursor during the synthesis process, resulting in the heterogeneous nucleation and incomplete carbonization of carbon spheres. In brief, the green-CDs samples exhibited not only graphite-like structure but also amorphous structure as similar to the blue-CDs samples. These findings imply that the structure of carbon cores should not be relate to green emission and blue emission. Despite extensive reports demonstrate that the internal nuclear structure with different degrees of defects largely affected the bandgaps in CDs [2,26,54,55], the explanation of the relationship between carbon defects and bandgaps is still obscure. As a matter of fact, research increasingly suggests that an alternative way to realize energy gap changes in CDs is tailoring their surface-related defective sites through surface composition [45,46,50,56].

To illustrate that size effects or surface states are the origin of green emission and blue emission, the particle size distribution of each CDs sample was measured and the corresponding histograms are displayed in Fig. 5 a–h. Specifically, the broad particle size distribution of each CDs sample clearly demonstrates that the size effect alone cannot be the limiting factor for the occurrence of different PL emissions. In addition, the average diameter from 1-CDs to 8-CDs were determined to be 6.80, 7.23, 6.80, 9.88, 8.83, 6.87, 16.37 and 10.70 nm, respectively. Such difference in average particle size of the as-prepared CDs further confirms that the green-CDs and blue-CDs with different emission behaviors do not conform to the quantum size effect. Therefore, it can be demonstrate that the different emission properties should be attributed to the surface states rather than the size effects.

The surface functional groups of the as-prepared CDs were investigated by FT-IR, as shown in Fig. S2. For the 6-CDs, since no acid participated in the synthesis of CDs, there are several common absorption bands can be observed from these nanostructures, including the stretching vibration for O−H (3427 cm−1), N−H (3220 cm−1), C−H (2931 and 2862 cm−1) and C=C/C=N (1630 cm−1), and the bending vibration for C−H (1490 cm−1) and C−N (1321 cm−1), which indicates the presence of conjugated aromatic structure together with O- and N-containing groups. Since the CH3COOH does not contain heteroatoms, the infrared absorption peak of 5-CDs was the same as that of 6-CDs. However, the participation of H3PO4 and HNO3 in the synthesis of CDs resulted in the introduction of new functional groups. The FT-IR spectra of 1-CDs, 2-CDs and 3-CDs showed almost no difference since the same starting materials. The characteristic absorption peaks at 1068 and 945 cm−1 can be assigned to the stretching vibration of P−O−R (R = aromatic structure) and P−O−H, respectively [4,57,58], indicating the doping of P atoms into CDs when these nanostructures were synthesized in H3PO4 aqueous solution. In the spectrum of 4-CDs, the emerging peaks at 1535 and 1382 cm−1 are assigned to the anti-symmetric and symmetric stretching vibration of −NO2. Additionally, the small absorption peak at 2582 cm−1 in the FTIR spectra of 1-CDs, 2-CDs, 3-CDs and 4-CDs corresponds to the stretching vibration of S−H, a small amount of S element in CDs might have been derived from impurities in H3PO4 and HNO3.

The FTIR spectra of 7-CDs and 8-CDs exhibited several characteristic absorption bands, The absorption peaks at about 1602 and 1161 cm−1 can be assigned to C=C and C−O−C, respectively. Additionally, the peak centered at 1401 cm−1 is ascribed to the symmetric stretching vibrations of the carboxylate groups on the surface of CDs. Significantly, the characteristic absorption peak at 2931 cm−1 is obscure for both 7-CDs and 8-CDs, revealing that the blue-CDs have almost no N−H stretching vibration. In addition, several P-containing groups, including P−O and P−O−R, were introduced in the 7-CDs due to the involvement of Na3PO4 in the synthesis reaction. For 8-CDs, the existence of a broad band at 1006 cm−1 in the FTIR spectrum indicates the stretching vibration of C−O. Despite the 1-CDs and 7-CDs were doped different phosphorus sources at the same synthesis condition, the FTIR results have revealed that their P-containing groups had almost no difference. Thus, the green and blue PL behaviors of CDs are independent of the P-containing groups. An identical result is also obtained by comparing all the synthesized green-emissive CDs samples. Based on the above analysis, the addition of different reagents in precursor result in CDs with either more O-containing groups or N-containing groups which ultimately determine their PL properties.

Elemental composition and speciation within the as-prepared CDs were determined by XPS. Fig. 6 represents the XPS spectra of 1-CDs, 2-CDs and 3-CDs, revealing that they were mainly composed of C, N, O and P elements with the binding energy peaks at 284, 399, 532, 191 and 133 eV corresponding to the C 1s, N 1s, O 1s, P 1s and P 2p, respectively. The high resolution of C 1s spectra, N 1s spectra and P 2p spectra of these three types of CDs showed almost no difference. In brief, the C 1s spectra could be de-convoluted into three peaks, at 284.5 eV (C=C/C−C), 285.1 eV (C−P/C−N) and 286.1 eV (C−O) [59,60]. The N 1s spectra can validate the existence of C−N=C (399.5 eV), N−(C) 3 (400.7 eV) and N−H (401.8 eV) in CDs [59,61]. The P 2p spectra can be fitted into two peaks, which are assigned to P−O (133.6 eV) and C−P (134.6 eV) groups [52]. Both the analyses of FTIR and XPS illustrate that the samples of 1-CDs, 2-CDs and 3-CDs were possessed of almost the same chemical composition in their structures. Moreover, the O to C ratio was 0.26, 0.39 and 0.54 for 1-CDs, 2-CDs and 3-CDs, respectively, the increase of ratio value implies the increasing oxygen content. It has been clearly demonstrated that the PL QY of 3-CDs, 2-CDs and 1-CDs was gradually increasing with the increase in synthesis temperature. Thus, the increased synthesis temperature is beneficial for the reduction of oxygen content in the CDs, which can significantly enhance the PL efficiency of CDs.

The XPS survey scan of 4-CDs, 5-CDs and 6-CDs are depicted in Fig. 7 . These types of CDs showed three apparent peaks at 284, 399, 532 eV corresponding to C 1s, N 1s and O 1s, respectively, indicating the 4-CDs, 5-CDs and 6-CDs were composed of same chemical elements in spite of their different starting materials. Due to the same nitrogen source were adopted to doping these CDs, the N 1s spectra of all types of green-CDs have almost no difference, while the C 1s spectra of 5-CDs and 6-CDs were differ from other types of green-CDs. Specifically, a new binding energy corresponding to O=C−O bond appeared in the C 1s spectra of 5-CDs and 6-CDs, revealing the presence of carboxyl groups within the structures of 5-CDs and 6-CDs.

Fig. 8 shows the XPS results of blue-CDs. The survey scan of 7-CDs and 8-CDs illustrate that both the two CDs samples contained C, O, N and Na. Specifically, the existence of Na stemmed from the residues of Na3PO4 and NaOH in the starting materials of 7-CDs and 8-CDs, respectively. In addition, similar to the green-emissive 1-CDs, a small amount of P element was also contained in blue-emissive 7-CDs (for the detailed elemental analysis see Table S2), which further indicates that the incorporation of P element in CDs is not responsible for green emission and blue emission. Notably, the peak associate with N 1s is much weaker than that of green-CDs, indicating a lower content of N element in blue-CDs. The high resolution XPS spectra of C 1s of all blue-CDs demonstrate that the O=C−O bond was incorporated in these nanostructures, and the amount of O=C−O in blue-CDs was greater than those in both 5-CDs and 6-CDs based on XPS results. In addition, although the N 1s spectrum of 7-CDs by Gaussian decomposition indicates that the C−N=C present along with a little N−H, the lower N content measured by XPS suggests that the 7-CDs had negligible amino groups on their surface. Moreover, the N 1s band of 8-CDs can be converted into N=C−N and N−(C) 3. Apparently, the N−H bond, which is present in all green-CDs, has almost no diagnostic signal in the N 1s spectra of 7-CDs and 8-CDs. This result is also confirmed by the corresponding FT-IR analysis. Therefore, it can be deduced that the surface-contained amino groups are intrinsic reason for changing surface states of CDs, and thus resulting in the green emission performance.

It is generally considered that the surface potential is highly dependent on the chemical composition, thus the zeta potential of each CDs sample was also determined to provide a further insight into the different PL properties. As tabulated in Table S3, the surface potential of all green-CDs samples in aqueous solution were positively charged, which can be attributed to the protonated amino groups on their surface. In contrast, the blue-CDs were possessed of negative charges due to their surface-contained negative ionized carboxyl groups. As a result, the different functional groups on the surface of CDs dominate the surface charge, and thus largely affect the PL emissions.

According to the experimental results, a mechanism model is proposed to provide a further insight into the different PL emission of as-prepared CDs (Fig. 9 ). The blue emission mechanism of CDs has been demonstrated by extensive reports in recent years. In most cases, the O-containing groups on sp2-hybridized carbons can induce significant local distortion and then create various energy levels in the π–π∗ gap. These energy gaps induced by O-containing groups (named O-related defect state) are more likely to be predominant due to the existence of C=O bonds. The electrons from the ground state are excited to the different vibrational energy levels of π∗ by different excitation. Subsequently, some excited electrons trapped by the corresponding O-related defect state, and finally transition back to the ground state through different radiative recombination paths, thus giving rise to the excitation-dependent blue emission (named LC1 emission). Indeed, the multiple O-containing groups and the non-existent amino groups result in the sample 8-CDs exhibits typical LC1 emission. However, the maximum emission of 7-CDs has been dramatically shifted to longer wavelength in comparison to that of 8-CDs. Such performance can be attributed to the P-containing groups in 7-CDs, the P-containing groups with strong electron-donating ability tend to create an energy-level state above the HOMO of the CDs (named P-related defect state). Thus, the LC2 emission which originate from the transition between O-related defect state and P-related defect state occur simultaneously with LC1 emission, presenting different PL behaviors in sample 7-CDs [62].

Compared with the blue emission theory of CDs, the evidence for the origin of green emission is still sparse. It has been demonstrated that the PL emission of the CDs with amino groups exhibits a redshift compared to that of the CDs without amino groups. Thus, the surface-contained amino groups play a leading role in the redshift of the PL emission. Many previous report have confirmed that the defect states which below the π∗ state can be affected by the electron-withdrawing or electron-donating abilities of functional groups. The protonated amino groups as electron donors have a capacity to increase electron density in CDs, resulting in a narrowing of the optical band gap [20,63]. Thus, the narrowed energy gap created by the protonated amino groups (named N-related defect state) in CDs is the intrinsic cause of green PL emission. When the excitation in short wavelength region, the electrons in the π orbitals of the luminescent conjugation unites can be excited to the π∗ orbitals. Then some excited electros in the π∗ orbitals may relax to the corresponding energy levels of O-related defect state, and finally recombine with the holes in the π orbitals, leading to the LC1 emission. For P doped green-CDs, including 1-CDs, 2-CDs and 3-CDs, the LC2 emission induced by the P-related defect state is also a part of PL emission under excitation at shorter wavelength. Both the LC1 emission and LC2 emission are characteristics of excitation-dependent performance. Meanwhile, other excited electrons relax to the N-related defect state, and eventually return to the π orbitals by means of radiation transitions (named LC3 emission). However, the longer excitation wavelengths facilitate electrons are excited and trapped directly by the N-related defect state. Then, the trapped electrons relax mainly through radiative ways, and thus resulting in long-wavelength fluorescent emission (named LC4 emission). Therefore, when the excitation wavelength is less than 400 nm, the LC1, LC2 and LC3 emission dominate the emission performance of green-CDs, thus leading to the emission wavelengths increased from 513 to 518 nm. When the excitation wavelength is longer than 400 nm, the energy of which is less than that needed for the π–π∗ transition, the electrons can only be excited to the N-related defect state. Accordingly, only the LC4 emission can be observed. It should be mentioned that the protonated amino groups is likely to induce a uniform N-related defect state, and thus resulting in a portion of green fluorescent emission with excitation-independence property.

Many previous reports have confirmed that the pH-dependent PL performance is one of the most important properties existed in CDs. Thus, exploring the mechanism of pH-dependent PL behaviors can offer valuable insights into the origin of PL emission for xylose-derived CDs. In this work, the sample 1-CDs was selected as representative green-CDs, and the pH value of 1-CDs solution was adjusted in the range of 3–11 using PBS buffer. The optical properties of 1-CDs in different pH solutions were investigated in detail to validate the proposed mechanism of green emission. As revealed in Fig. 10 , the PL performances were different in two pH regions, the optimal excitation and emission were centered at 442 nm and 518 nm, respectively, without any change as the pH increased from 3 to 7. However, the excitation–emission matrix for 1-CDs exhibited evolution upon increasing the pH to 8, the optimal excitation and emission shifted to 375 nm and 500 nm, respectively, suggesting a blue-shifted performance. These findings suggest that the 1-CDs had different maximum transitions in different pH ranges. On the basis of mechanism analysis, the LC4 emission from N-related defected state dominate the PL behavior of 1-CDs at pH 3–7, while the LC1 emission from O-related defect state and LC2 emission from P-related defect state are predominant when the pH in the region of 8–11.

For further demonstrate that the pH-dependent PL performance is related to the evolution of defect states, it is necessary to investigate the variation of PL intensities with the increasing of pH value under the excitation at 360 and 440 nm, respectively. As depicted in Fig. 11 a, when the 1-CDs were excited by different excitation wavelengths, there were different variation laws in the PL intensities with the increasing of pH value. The PL intensities obtained under 360 nm excitation decreased initially with the pH increased from 3 to 5, while the further increasing of pH will result in significant increase in PL intensities. However, the pH-dependent PL intensities behavior obtained at the excitation wavelength of 440 nm showed an opposite trend to that obtained under 360 nm excitation. Specifically, the PL intensities determined at the excitation wavelength of 440 nm increased gradually with the increasing of pH, and the PL intensity of the CDs was maximal at pH 5, and then declined significantly with the increase in pH value. These findings suggest that pH-dependent PL performance associate with excitation wavelength. To support this hypothesis, the emission spectra of 1-CDs in different pH solutions were recorded under 360 nm and 440 nm excitation, respectively. As shown in Fig. 11b, the emission spectra of 1-CDs in different pH solutions demonstrate a gradual evolution of PL performance under 360 nm excitation. With the pH increased from 3 to 8, the peak position of PL spectra shifted from 518 to 497 nm, while the further increasing of pH value had almost no effect on the peak position. In contrast, the emission peaks obtained under 440 nm excitation was almost insusceptible to the variation of pH (Fig. 11c). Since the emissions from multiple LCs are dependent on the excitation wavelengths, the variation of the PL intensities and emission spectra under different pH conditions should be related to the change of defect states.

The difference in PL behaviors due to changes in defect states can be further illustrated by absorption spectroscopy. As depicted in Fig. 12 , the UV–vis spectra of 1-CDs at different pH conditions were measured. The absorption band at 285 nm and 385 nm were not changed with the increasing of pH, while the broad absorption band at about 440 nm became weak, and finally disappeared at higher pH, such spectral variation indicates that the increase in pH gradually eliminate the N-related defect state. These findings reinforce evidences that the pH-dependent PL performance is related to the evolution of defect states.

According to the above analyses, an explanation on the pH-dependent PL performance is proposed as follows: the defects or disordered locations of low-dimensional materials tend to bind excitons and charge carriers [64,65]. Thus, doping H+ is equivalent to injecting holes into the defect states of carbon quantum dots, the photogenerated electrons may be trapped by these holes, thus leading to the decrease of charge transition probability from the ground state to the defect states. For the N-related defect state, the amount of protonated amino groups can reach the maximum value at pH = 5, and the corresponding PL intensity is the largest at the excitation wavelength of 440 nm. The further decreasing pH has almost no effect on the protonated amino groups, while the number of injected holes will increase in the N-related defected state, leading to a slight decrease in the LC4 emission. When the pH increase from 5 to 11, the protonated amino groups gradually lose protons, and finally decompose at higher pH. Accordingly, the PL intensity obtained from the LC4 emission decrease sharply with the increase in pH from 5 to 11. Moreover, since LC4 emission is predominant in 1-CDs under 440 nm excitation, the emission peaks are almost insusceptible to the pH variation. Unlike N-related defect state, the amount of holes localized in the O-related defect state or P-related defect state decrease gradually with the increasing of pH which is then favorable for the recombination of electron-hole pairs. Such an increase in charge transition is likely to result in the enhancement of LC1 and LC2 emission. We have known that the multiple LC emission is dependent on the excitation wavelengths, the shorter excitation wavelengths are beneficial for the LC1, LC2 and LC3 emission, the increasing of pH in the range of 3–8 facilitates the LC1 and LC2 emission, while the LC3 emission is hindered simultaneously, one possible reason for this transformation is that the O-related defect state is more likely to capture electrons than the N-related defect state with the increase in pH value under 360 nm excitation. Thus, the LC1, LC2 and LC3 emission play a combined role in the blueshift of emission peaks and fluctuation of PL intensities in the pH region of 3–8. As the pH is adjusted from 8 to 13, the emission from LC1 and LC2 dominate the PL behavior of 1-CDs completely and the corresponding PL intensities increase significantly, while the LC3 has no contribution to the PL emission.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Xylose-derived CDs ink for anti-counterfeit printing
ce:section.ce:para: The PL performance of 1-CDs were pH-dependent with two distinct emitted color under UV light. As shown in Fig. 13 , the 1-CDs solutions with pH value in the range of 3–7 emitted green luminescence, while the cyan emitting was observed at pH 8–11. Such a transformation of emitted color in two pH ranges indicates that the 1-CDs aqueous solution can be used as fluorescent ink, enabling the fabrication of optical anti-counterfeit labels and encryption of written information. To better demonstrate the practical application of the green emitting 1-CDs-based anti-counterfeiting ink, the aqueous 1-CDs solution was subjected to thermal treatment to assess the emission stability. As seen in Fig. S3, the PL intensity showed very small fluctuation when the treatment temperature increased from 0 °C to 200 °C, and the emission color had no change even the heating temperature was up to the 200 °C (inset in Fig. S3). The above-mentioned results demonstrate that the 1-CDs-based ink is suitable for either inkjet printing or offset printing to meet the requirement of commercial application. Furthermore, the synthesized 1-CDs were irradiated by UV light (365 nm) for 300 min to investigate the photobleaching effect, it is clear that the PL intensity exhibited negligible variation along with the exposure time (Fig. S4), the inset of Fig. S4 illustrates that the emission color of 1-CDs ink was actually insensitive to the irradiation time. Thus, it is reasonable to conclude that the 1-CDs-based ink with high stability is conducive to the reliability of anti-counterfeiting.

Fig. 14 a presents a fluorescent QR code printed with 1-CDs ink on a filter paper, the printed pattern displayed golden yellow color under natural irradiation. As expected, the pattern coated with 1-CDs exhibited bright green fluorescence under UV lamp (Fig. 14b), when the filter paper was brushed by dilute NaOH solution, cyan emission was clearly observed under UV excitation (Fig. 14c). To our delight, the cyan pattern can be restored to green emission after dilute CH3COOH coating (Fig. 14d). Additionally, the word “Carbon Dots” was also printed on filter paper with an inkjet printer using 1-CDs ink, as seen from the photographs in Fig. S5, the word Carbon Dots have a reversible pattern transformation coinciding with the printed fluorescent QR code when the 1-CDs coated information was sprayed with dilute NaOH solution and CH3COOH solution. More importantly, the photographs of printed pattern in Fig. S6 illustrate that the fluorescent reversibility of printed information was stable even after 7 days, thus demonstrating that the prepared 1-CDs-based ink is possessed of excellent durability. To further demonstrate the chemical stability of the switchable fluorescence for anti-counterfeiting information, the patterns of “Carbon Dots” were treated by several common bleachers including soda lime, ethanol and methanol (see Figure S7 (a-c)). It is clear that all the printed patterns gave intensive green emission after contact with these bleachers and also displayed fluorescent reversibility as triggered by pH changing. Overall, the green-emissive 1-CD-based inks with convenient preparation, fluorescence transformation, excellent designability and high chemical stability would open a new door for optical anti-counterfeiting applications.

In summary, high-efficient green-emissive CDs were synthesized from a precursor comprising xylose and mPD in H3PO4 solution through a facile microwave-assisted hydrothermal method. To investigate the contribution of P-, N- and O-containing groups to the green emission performance, the synthesis temperature and the precursor coupled with HNO3, CH3COOH, Na3PO4 and NaOH were selected as models to synthesize xylose-derived CDs for a systematical study on the PL properties. It was found that the precursor coupled with and without acid have capacity to introduce amino groups on the surface of CDs and thus result in their green emission behavior, while the alkaline environment of the starting solution help to create the multiple O-containing groups within the structure of resultant CDs, and thus leading to the blue emission performance. The existence of P-containing groups and the increasing of synthesis temperature has been determined to be beneficial to improve the PL QY of green-CDs. These surface-contained multiple functional groups which dominate different LCs of green emissive CDs were largely affected by pH, Benefiting from the switching between green and blue emission in different pH regions, the synthesized xylose-derived CDs with high-efficient green emission were employed as fluorescent ink to fabricate optical anti-counterfeit QR code and word information. The resulting printed pattern with tunable emission performance, which together with its high chemical stability enables new research opportunities for the application of the xylose-derived CDs. Furthermore, the success of this pilot will pave a way for value-added utilization of xylose, as well as providing a facile and green strategy for large-scale preparation of CDs. We believe that the different doping process will endow the xylose-derived CDs with multiple PL performances, and thus providing tremendous application potential for detection, catalyst and bioimaging.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 31870549), the Jiangsu Nature Science Foundation (BK20161524), the Program for 333 Talents Project in Jiangsu Province (Grant No. BRA2016381), the Postgraduate Research &Practice Innovation Program of Jiangsu Province (KYCX17_0839), the Doctorate Fellowship Foundation of Nanjing Forestry University (2017) and the Advanced Analysis and Testing Center of Nanjing Forestry University.
